channel.title: The Modal Future
channel.description: Audio essays on frontier technology. Each subject gets two episodes: a primer on how the machine is built and who builds it, then a thesis on where it is going, with dates. The first subject is AI compute.
channel.itunes:author: The Modal Future

item.title: Thesis: What the Build-Out Is Actually Betting On
item.description: The bears point at the glut — the capex, the debt, the chips that wear out in three years. The bulls point at capability — the benchmark scores climbing every month. Both are watching the wrong number. The whole trillion-dollar build-out rests on a single quantity almost nobody prices: how much capability the next dollar of reinforcement-learning practice actually buys. The committed view of where the machine settles over the next decade — what breaks first, what would prove it wrong, and the one thing to watch that isn't a benchmark. The finale of the AI-compute series.

item.title: Primer: How a Frontier Model Is Actually Trained
item.description: The training run is a months-long computation across tens of thousands of chips that fails every few hours and must not die. Ninety percent of the compute trains models you will never see. The newest and most valuable stage is mostly the model talking to itself. And the one thing the labs will not publish is the recipe. A tour of how intelligence is actually manufactured — the experiments, the failures, and the reinforcement-learning gym. Part eight of the AI-compute series.

item.title: Primer: Inside an AI Data Center
item.description: An AI data center is no longer a box that houses computers. It is a machine for moving heat and electricity — and it is the one part of the whole system that does not get cheaper over time. Why air can no longer cool the chips, why the power has to be rewired, why a building designed in 2024 is already wrong for the rack arriving in 2027, and why the equipment to fix it carries a fifteen-billion-dollar backlog. How the building works. Part six of the AI-compute series.

item.title: Primer: How Chips Become One Computer
item.description: A single chip can't hold a frontier AI model, so the machine is built from tens of thousands wired together — and the wiring, not the chips, is now the hard part. Inside the rack: a fabric called NVLink, two miles of copper, and a wall that copper hits in 2028. Between racks: ninety-eight thousand lasers, the fight between InfiniBand and Ethernet, and why only about forty percent of a cluster's compute ever turns into work. How chips become one computer. Part five of the AI-compute series.
